title: Why Does Office Politics Exist Everywhere?

situation.content:
You joined this team because of the mission. The work is interesting. The people are smart. And yet — somehow — you spend 30% of your energy navigating who's allied with whom, who's angling for what, and which conversations are really about what they claim to be about.

You've tried to stay above it. "I just want to do good work." But the people who say that most loudly are often the ones most blindsided when the work alone doesn't protect them.

story.content:
In the Kalahari Desert, meerkat colonies operate with a political sophistication that would make any corporate strategist uncomfortable.

Each colony has a dominant pair — the alpha female and male who produce most of the offspring. But their position isn't maintained through strength alone. It's maintained through a web of alliances, surveillance, and strategic violence that researchers have spent decades mapping.

The dominant female doesn't just lead. She actively suppresses reproduction in subordinate females — evicting pregnant subordinates from the group, sometimes killing their pups. This isn't cruelty. It's resource politics: the colony can only feed so many mouths, and the dominant female ensures her genetic line gets priority.

Subordinate females, in turn, form coalitions. They babysit each other's pups (building alliance capital), groom the dominant female (signaling submission to avoid eviction), and wait — sometimes for years — for a moment of weakness. When the dominant female is injured or aging, a coalition can overthrow her. The transition is rarely peaceful.

Meerkats also have sentinels — individuals who watch for predators while others forage. This looks altruistic. But research shows sentinels tend to take the role when they've already eaten enough — and the elevated position gives them first sight of danger, meaning they're often the first to flee. The "selfless" act is also self-serving.

Here's why this matters for understanding office politics: meerkats don't have culture, ideology, or moral frameworks. They have scarce resources, social hierarchy, and genetic self-interest. And they produce politics — coalitions, surveillance, strategic generosity, positioning, and power transitions — as naturally as they breathe.

Politics isn't a human invention. It isn't a failure of character or culture. It's what happens whenever finite resources meet social creatures who need each other to survive. It's physics — the inevitable result of multiple agents with different interests sharing a constrained system.

The office isn't political because people are corrupt. It's political because resources (promotions, budget, attention, credit) are finite, people have different interests, and the system requires cooperation between people who are also competing. That's the definition of politics. It predates language.
story.title: The Story

contrast.content: In the Panchatantra, the ancient Indian collection of fables, there's a tale of crows at war with owls. The crows are losing badly — the owls raid at night when crows are blind. So the crow leader devises a plan: he publicly attacks and "exiles" his own minister in a staged argument. The minister, appearing disgraced, defects to the owls, earns their trust over months, and eventually reveals the location of their roost. The crows attack and win. Politics here isn't vice — it's survival technology. The fable doesn't moralize about deception. It teaches that understanding political dynamics is essential for survival — and pretending they don't exist is the fastest way to lose.
contrast.title: The Contrast

principle.content:
Politics isn't a corruption of good systems — it's what happens whenever finite resources meet social creatures. It's physics, not pathology.

The choice isn't between "being political" and "just doing good work." The choice is between navigating politics skillfully and being navigated by people who do. Refusing to see the game doesn't exempt you from it — it just means you're playing blind.

psychology.content:
Social psychologists distinguish between two types of political behavior: "self-serving politics" (advancing personal interests at others' expense) and "relationship politics" (building alliances, understanding stakeholder interests, creating coalitions for shared goals). Most people condemn the first while practicing the second without recognizing it as political.

Research on organizational behavior shows that political skill — defined as the ability to read social situations, build networks, and influence others — is a stronger predictor of career success than technical competence in most organizations (Ferris et al., 2007). This isn't because organizations are broken. It's because organizations are social systems where coordination, influence, and alignment are genuinely necessary work.

The psychological trap is the "just world" belief: if I do excellent work, I'll be recognized and rewarded. This is comforting but empirically wrong. Excellent work that nobody knows about, that threatens the wrong person, or that isn't aligned with decision-makers' priorities won't advance — no matter how good it is. Understanding this isn't cynicism. It's literacy.
psychology.title: Why It Happens

move.content:
1. Accept politics as physics, not pathology. Saying "I don't do politics" is like saying "I don't do gravity." You're in it whether you engage consciously or not. The question is whether you navigate intentionally or get navigated.

2. Map the system. Who makes decisions? What do they care about? Who influences them? What resources are scarce? Understanding the power map isn't manipulation — it's the prerequisite for effective action. You can't change a system you refuse to see.

3. Build coalitions around shared interests. Meerkat politics isn't just dominance — it's alliance. Find people whose interests genuinely overlap with yours. Build relationships before you need them. The time to develop trust with stakeholders is when nothing is at stake.

4. Make your work visible to the right people. Excellent work in a vacuum is invisible work. This isn't bragging — it's responsible communication. The people who decide your future can't reward what they don't know about. Brief the right people. Share credit visibly. Narrate your impact.

5. Distinguish between ethical influence and manipulation. Politics becomes toxic when it's zero-sum ("for me to win, you must lose") or deceptive. Ethical politics is positive-sum: building coalitions where multiple parties benefit, surfacing information that helps decision-makers, and advocating for outcomes you genuinely believe in. The goal isn't to be apolitical — it's to be politically effective without becoming someone you dislike.
move.title: The Move
